item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this is supposed to tie into other scp lore that states the Xia dynasty did exist but was very anomalous and so evidence of it was suppressed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In curious where SCP-2880-150 is. After all, they're all paired, right?

item.title: Wikiwalk Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
edit: edits made

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ Show
- Hide

To link to another Hawaiian inspired skip, I propose the following edit:

SCP-2880-B controlled a large number of western Pacific islands between Taiwan and Hawai'i from about 2500 BCE to 1700 BCE

to

SCP-2880-B controlled a large number of western Pacific islands between Taiwan and Hawai'i from about 2500 BCE to 1700 BCE

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this. The mystery of the culture behind the objects makes it very interesting. +1

item.title: Re: Lacks Presence
item.content:encoded: Now, I don't want to go down the route of saying "you don't like it 'cause you don't understand it", because that's just going to end badly, but you have to keep in mind that the teleporters themselves aren't the important bit, the main thing is the Savaki culture and its story. (which, to be fair, prominently features teleporters.)

item.title: Re: Containment Protocals
item.content:encoded:
The first paragraph is a good point. I might edit something like that in when I have the time.

The second paragraph presupposes that the Foundation knows exactly what overuse of the objects will ultimately lead to, which the Foundation doesn't, and even then it would be a little over the top.

item.title: Containment Protocals
item.content:encoded:
How do you keep 149 bowls, which are themselves broken into many smaller fragments in a single storage locker? They need a dedicated archive, maybe with a passing reference to meticulous organization and labeling.

More importantly, the fact that the Foundation deliberately keeps them in a disassembled state is an opportunity for some creating a sense of foreboding, but right now it doesn't hit that point quite right. Consider changing the tone to make it a bit more authoritative and restrictive. Perhaps each instance of SCP-288-A has one of its fragments stored in a different location which keeps the available plates under the critical level of reconstruction. Perhaps you need a higher level of clearance or need to request permission from the lead researcher.

item.title: Lacks Presence
item.content:encoded: Am I the only one that finds this skip underwhelming? We already have a couple teleporters and if this one wants to stand it still needs work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Here's a hint: Their destruction is related to the hallucinations that the devices cause, and related to, but not directly caused by, the conflict with other Polynesian groups. And I feex.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pretty interesting; nice to see a more archaeological oriented SCP.
A short comment, only: it says there that there's this site that "regularly traded with the Xia dynasty"; however, I don't think in archaeological contexts the cultures of neolithic China are referred as Xia, but rather as Longshan for example (or as some of the other numerous neolithic cultures there were in China at the time).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thoroughly enjoyed; the remains of an ancient, technological society that discovered teleportation, only to be wiped out by unknown assailants (maybe other humans, maybe Daevites, maybe…) rubs me in all the right ways.

One glitch:

Provisional Area 2880-B3 …Recovered artifacts indicate that SCP-2880-A regularly traded with the Xia dynasty, Middle Jomon peoples, and the Indus Valley Civilization

Shouldn't that be SCP-2880-B?

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The COOLNESS! Great job!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The only thing that I wish was that column 3 of the table was just a bit wider so the list of -AX objects recovered was simply stacked, and not rippling down and hyphenating through the thin column provided, so:
•SCP-2880-A1
•SCP-2880-A2
•SCP-2880-A3
•SCP-2880-A16

I thought you did a good job of upping the unknown just through progressively describing the provisional areas. A +1 has been transported to your rating module!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this but parts of it are worded poorly. I don't know if it's just me but it took a few times reading the description to get what you meant (at first I thought you had to reconstruct one of the devices to 90% and then it would teleport you to the other 10%, it didn't really make it clear that they came in pairs that were linked to each other). The molars from headless skeletons thing also confused me, I got what you meant but it was worded awkwardly.

I never saw the version with the Daevite reference but I would have actually liked it, since I go for that kind of intertextuality, although I'm probably in the minority here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At first, I thought you finally ditched the Daevite reference… Then I read through the comments and… Well, I did warn you about it.

But now when I think about the last bit, the ambiguity can be taken to refer to the Daevites… or something else altogether…

Note: I strongly question if Indus Valley and Xia Dynasty had the means and capacity for trans-oceanic trade since they're both land-based cultures without access to the Pacific Ocean. I would say Proto-Malays make it more realistic to the seafaring culture… unless SCP-2880 allow them to even go inland to India and China… which is anomalous and fit in… now that I think about it…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In that case I'll change it to a reference to the Indus Valley Civilization.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ok, I concede. The conquerors seemed pretty Daevite-ish, but it might be best to leave it even more ambiguous than it is now.1 A connection like that doesn't really have full impact as a one-line reference anyway; maybe it should be left to a tale or something?

Footnotes
1. I acknowledge that it's already pretty ambiguous, but, that's all besides the point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's an asston of skips that existed during that time period that could have theoretically interacted with the phenomenon here. But why shoehorn that stuff in when it adds nothing to the inherent narrative of the piece? It's clutter at best, transparently mugging for upvotes at worst (I do believe that it's the former in this particular case).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Voting for leaving it; Daevites had a pretty big stretch of territory to call their own. I don't see why they wouldn't have interacted with these guys. My only nitpick? SCP-2880-A-15 was apparently found twice.

Edit: In Areas B1 and B4, specifically.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've reworded the bit about the skeletons to be less confusing (The skulls were removed, but were nearby) and I'm currently debating whether to remove or leave in the Daevite reference.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Cool object, cool effect, cool purpose, and I very much like that you made a whole society to go along with it.

Oh, I'm glad Kalinin asked about the molars, I noticed that as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not bad. Enough detail to sell the phenomenon as a genuinely documented scientific phenomenon, without going overboard and boring the reader with extraneous material. It hits a sweet spot in terms of tone, dry enough to help sell the more unsettling bits of the log without treading into "oooOOooo boogedy boooo" territory. Good work.

Two issues though, which while not affecting my overall vote I would really like to see addressed:

  • How can they extract DNA from the molars of the skeletons if they're all headless?
  • There is no reason whatsoever for that Daevite reference to be in there. I understand that crosslinking is being pushed now in some quarters, and that's fine and all, but I would prefer that stuff like that serve some sort of purpose. This article is strong enough to stand on its own; I see no reason to gratuitously throw cross-references in there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it! Very specific & the tone is perfect, which is what really pulls this off. The object itself could have been mediocre/boring if not for the added detail about saline water and hallucinations of drowning etc. after repeated use. Perfect tone & just enough going on here to make you wonder = upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My Polynesian teleporter skip is finally done!

I'd like to thank MrWrong, TwistedGears, Dillinger PhD, Darbot, DoctorDoomsday, Metaphysician, A Random Day, Themanofum, Gbroxey, Eskobar, Dr. Leonard, Prior and Decibelle for reviewing this in the chat, and MadCatUSA for reviewing this in the forums.

The image is a modified version of an image from here.
